methods.name: Stage-Progression

methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Zap Energy, a Series D nuclear energy company that has raised $330M+, is valued within a frontier sector containing three public comparables (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and five private peers including Oklo, X-Energy, Nano Nuclear Energy, Fuse Energy, and Radiant. The fragmented maturity profile of this cohort—spanning exploratory through advanced development stages—limits the applicability of direct peer multiples.

Stage-progression methodology, calibrated to typical Series D step-ups, applies a 1.5x multiplier to the $330M total raised proxy, yielding $495M. This represents the single applicable triangulation method; peer-median analysis was not executable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting minimum count thresholds (0 of 3 required). The broader nuclear energy comp set exists but lacks sufficient homogeneity for reliable multiple extraction at Zap's specific stage and capital profile.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $421M (low), $495M (median), to $569M (high), anchored primarily to stage-progression mechanics rather than market multiples. Key uncertainties center on the absence of narrow peer benchmarks, the early-stage risk profile characteristic of frontier nuclear technology, and execution risk inherent to pre-commercial-scale operations. Valuation confidence would materially improve with either comparable Series D nuclear closings or public market data from similarly capitalized peers.
